person.name: Richard de Trafford Lord of Trafford and Stretford

nameForms.fullText: Richard de Trafford Lord of Trafford and Stretford

person.userCertifiedLifeSummary:
«b»Biography«/b»
In the reign of King John, he received lands from Hugh Massey and his daughter Margery, viz. the whole lordship of Stretford, to be held of William de Ferrers, Earl of Derby. He also obtained a grant of lands in Stretford from Richard fitzAde de Urmston, as well as lands in other places.

"In the troubled times of the Baronial wars with King John, he divided his land between his elder son Henry and younger son Geoffrey. Henry got the lands of Trafford, Stretford and the whole of the lands near Manchester (which had then become of considerable extent), while Geoffrey received the manors of Chadderton (Catherton) and Foxdenton." 

Court record of 3 Nov 1221 - To the sheriff of Lancaster. Richard son of Henry of Trafford has made fine with the king by 20s. for his relief of the land formerly of Henry, his father, whose heir he is, which Richard ought to hold of the king, for which he has terms at the Exchequer. Order that, having accepted security from Richard for rendering the aforesaid fine to the king, he is to cause him to have full seisin without delay of all lands that he ought to hold from the king in his bailiwick and that are in the king?s hand. Witness H. de Burgh etc."

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

nameMeanings.name: Trafford
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from Trafford (Lancashire), Trafford in Chipping Warden (Northamptonshire), and Bridge, Mickle, and Wimbolds Trafford (Cheshire). The Northamptonshire placename probably derives from Old English træppe ‘trap’ + ford ‘ford’. The Cheshire placenames derive from Old English trōg ‘trough’ + ford. The Lancashire placename is a development of Stretford (Lancashire); most cases of the surname probably derive from this place. Early members of the de Trafford family, recorded here from the 13th century, were also recorded in medieval documents as de Stratford(e). Both the surname and the placename lost the initial S-. The manor of Trafford was carved out of Stretford township, and perhaps because its manor house was located at some distance from Stretford village, Trafford was adopted for the new settlement from an early date. See also Stratford .

variants.name: Stratford
meanings: habitational name from any of various places called Stratford, for example in Bedfordshire, Buckinghamshire, Middlesex, Northamptonshire, Suffolk, Warwickshire, and Wiltshire, from Old English strǣt ‘Roman road’ (see Street ) + ford ‘ford’.

habitational name principally from Strafford Sands (now lost) in Mexborough (Yorkshire), presumed to be the ancient meeting-place of Strafforth Wapentake, and deriving from the same source as 1 above.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard de Trafford Lord of Trafford and Stretford was born before 1200, in Trafford Manor, Lancashire, England, his father, Henry I fitzRobert de Trafford, was 20 and his mother, Avice, was 17. He married Christiana Chetham in 1223, in England. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 1 daughter. He died about 1272, in England.

Il a épousé Christiana Chetham en 1223, à Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé environ 1272, à Angleterre.

ko.lifeSummary: Richard de Trafford Lord of Trafford and Stretford(이)가 1200년 이전 영국 랭카실르 트래퍼드 먼어에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Henry I fitzRobert de Trafford(은)는 20세였고, 그의 어머니 Avice(은)는 17세였다. 그는 1223년 영국에서 Christiana Chetham(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 대략 1272년 영국에서 사망했다.
